items.title: The Day Bao Found Us
items.author: Norman Yung
items.excerpt: He was sitting at the edge of the market, watching everyone pass. Nobody stopped. Until one rainy afternoon, we did.
items.body:
He was sitting at the edge of the market, watching everyone pass. Nobody stopped - not the vendors, not the tourists, not the locals on their scooters. Just a thin brown dog with hopeful eyes and a slightly crooked ear.

We almost walked by too. But something about the way he tilted his head made us turn around.

A slow kind of trust

Stray dogs do not trust easily, and they should not have to. It took three days of leaving food at the same corner before Bao let me sit near him. A week before he ate from my hand. A month before he climbed into the back of the truck on his own.

Rescue is not a single dramatic moment. It is a hundred small ones, stacked on top of each other until trust becomes possible.

Today Bao sleeps on a cushion by the door and snores louder than anyone I have ever met. He still watches the street - old habits - but now he watches it from the safe side of the glass.

items.title: Feeding Routes and Why They Matter
items.author: Norman Yung
items.excerpt: Consistency is everything. A reliable feeding route keeps a colony healthy and makes medical care possible.
items.body:
Every evening I drive the same loop: the temple, the canal bridge, the empty lot behind the school, and finally the night market. Same stops, same time, same order. The dogs have learned the rhythm, and the rhythm is the whole point.

Why routine works

A predictable feeding route does more than fill bellies. It lets you track who is healthy, who is limping, who is pregnant, and who has gone missing. It turns a scattered group of strays into a colony you can actually care for.

When a dog needs medicine, you already know where to find them. When a new face shows up, you notice immediately. Consistency is the quiet backbone of street-level rescue.

Building your own route

Start small. Pick two or three spots and visit them at the same time every day. The dogs will learn your schedule within a week. From there, you can expand. But the key is never to skip a day without good reason - the dogs are counting on you, even if they do not show it yet.

items.title: Spay, Neuter, and the Kindest Math There Is
items.author: Norman Yung
items.excerpt: One unspayed female and her offspring can lead to thousands of dogs in a few years. Prevention is the most compassionate tool we have.
items.body:
It is not the most heartwarming topic, but it might be the most important one on this blog. The single biggest thing we can do for stray dogs is not rescuing one at a time - it is preventing the next generation from being born onto the street.

The numbers

A community spay-and-neuter program steadily shrinks the population without harming a single animal. Fewer puppies born outside means fewer dogs starving, fewer hit by traffic, and more resources for the ones already here.

Compassion sometimes looks like a vet clinic and a spreadsheet.

If you can only support one cause in animal welfare, support trap-neuter-return. It is the kindest math there is.

items.title: What a Wagging Tail Actually Means
items.author: Norman Yung
items.excerpt: A wag is not always happiness. Reading a stray's body language correctly can keep both of you safe.
items.body:
People assume a wagging tail means a happy dog. With strays, it is more complicated - and reading it wrong can get you bitten or scare off a dog who needed your help.

Watch the whole body

A loose, low wag with a relaxed mouth is friendly. A high, stiff wag paired with a frozen body is a warning. Yawning, lip-licking, and turning away are signs of stress, not rudeness. Let the dog set the pace.

The best thing you can do is get low, go slow, and never reach over a strange dog's head. Patience reads as safety, and safety is what they are starving for.

items.title: Packing a Street Rescue Kit
items.author: Norman Yung
items.excerpt: What I keep in the truck at all times - because you never know when the next dog will need you.
items.body:
You cannot help if you are not ready. After a few missed opportunities, I built a kit that lives in the truck permanently. Here is what is in it.

The essentials

Slip lead and a spare. A few cans of strong-smelling food. Bottled water and a collapsible bowl. Old towels. Basic first aid - gauze, saline, blunt scissors. Gloves. A blanket for the crate. And the phone number of a vet who answers after hours.

Preparation turns panic into a plan.

None of it is fancy. All of it has mattered.

items.title: Adopt, Don't Shop - With Your Eyes Open
items.author: Norman Yung
items.excerpt: Adoption changes a life, but a good match matters as much as a good heart. A few honest questions before you commit.
items.body:
"Adopt, don't shop" is a great slogan, and I believe in it completely. But a slogan is not a plan. A former street dog comes with a history you cannot fully see, and the best adoptions start with honesty.

Ask yourself first

Do you have time for a dog who may need months to decompress? Patience for accidents, fear, and the occasional chewed shoe? A budget for vet care? If yes, you are ready - and there is a dog out there who will love you with their entire being.

Go in with your eyes open and your heart will follow.

items.title: Midnight on the Canal Bridge
items.author: Norman Yung
items.excerpt: She had been living under the bridge for weeks. Getting her out took patience, sardines, and one very long night.
items.body:
The call came at 10pm. Someone had spotted a limping dog under the old canal bridge - the same one locals had been talking about for weeks. By the time I got there, it was nearly midnight.

Under the bridge

She was tucked into a gap between the concrete pilings, barely visible. Small, maybe 12 kilos, with matted fur and one front paw held off the ground. She watched me with eyes that said "I have been disappointed before."

I set a tin of sardines three meters away and sat down on the cold concrete. And waited.

The long game

It took four hours. Four hours of quiet, of not making eye contact, of pushing the tin slightly closer every twenty minutes. At 2am she finally ate. At 3am she let me loop the lead over her neck. By dawn she was at the emergency vet, getting her paw cleaned and wrapped.

Some rescues happen in seconds. The ones that matter most usually take all night.

We named her Bridge. She lives with a retired couple now who let her sleep on the couch and never rush her when she needs a moment. She still flinches at loud sounds, but she no longer hides.

items.title: Understanding Dog Colony Dynamics
items.author: Norman Yung
items.excerpt: Street dogs are not lone wolves. They form complex social groups with rules, hierarchies, and surprising cooperation.
items.body:
One of the biggest misconceptions about street dogs is that they are solitary. In reality, most form loose colonies - social groups with their own territories, hierarchies, and even something resembling friendship.

How colonies form

A colony usually centers on a food source: a market, a restaurant, a generous neighbor. Dogs drift in, and over weeks they sort out who belongs. There is a pecking order, but it is more fluid than people expect. A dog near the top one month may slide down after an illness.

What this means for helpers

When you feed a colony, you are working with a social system. Remove one dog suddenly and the whole balance shifts. This is why TNR (trap-neuter-return) emphasizes the return: keep the colony stable, just smaller over time.

Watch long enough and you will see dogs grooming each other, sharing shade, and standing guard while others sleep. It is not the wolf pack of documentaries. It is something quieter and more cooperative.

items.title: The First Vet Visit: What to Expect
items.author: Norman Yung
items.excerpt: Taking a street dog to the vet for the first time is stressful for everyone. Here is how to make it easier.
items.body:
The first vet visit with a street dog is nothing like bringing in a pet who has been handled their whole life. Everything is new, everything is terrifying, and your job is to be the calm in the middle of it.

Before you go

Get the dog comfortable with a crate or carrier at home first. Put treats inside and let them explore it on their terms. On the day, skip breakfast so they are motivated by food rewards at the clinic.

At the clinic

Ask for the first appointment of the day when the waiting room is empty. Bring a towel that smells like home. Let the vet know this is a former street dog - good vets will adjust their approach, moving slower, using less restraint, and letting the dog sniff everything first.

A good first vet visit sets the tone for every visit after. Take your time.

Expect a full checkup: heart, lungs, teeth, skin, ears, and bloodwork. Most street dogs have parasites - it is normal and treatable. The vet will set up a deworming and vaccination schedule. Breathe. You are both going to be fine.
